segments.s606-g65: خروشی (a noun) is the subject meaning a cry/roar; برآمد is a past/present literary verb 'arose/issued forth' placed after the noun (typical Persian V‑final order is relaxed in poetry). هم‌انگه is an adverbial time phrase 'at that moment/then' modifying برآمد, and ز + در is the poetic prepositional phrase 'from the door/threshold' giving the source of the sound.
segments.s606-g66: که‌ای here functions as a vocative cluster (که + ای) introducing direct address — effectively 'O' (it links the shout to who is addressed). پهلوانان is the vocative plural 'heroes/champions'. زرین modifies کمر as a compound adjective-noun 'golden‑girdled' (زرین کمر = 'with golden belts'). The phrase is a short vocative: 'O champions, (you) of golden girdles.'
segments.s606-g67: به introduces an adverbial phrase 'in/according to'; اندازه‌بر here names the measure or measuring standard and functions adverbially ('in proportion/according to the measure'); هرکسی is 'each person' or 'everyone', and می‌خورید is 2nd person plural present/gnomic 'you receive/you will get' — in this moralizing line it means 'you will be dealt with' or 'you will get (what is due)'. The word order places the adverbial measure phrase before the verb for emphasis.
segments.s606-g68: به آغاز و فرجام خود is a prepositional phrase 'to/at your beginning and end' where خود is the reflexive/possessive 'your own'. بنگرید is the (literary) 2nd person plural imperative/subjunctive 'look', 'consider' — here an exhortation to reflect on one's origin and outcome. The coordinate pair آغاز و فرجام (beginning and end) is the object of بنگرید.
segments.s606-g69: چو is the temporal conjunction 'when'. می‌تان here is mi + ‑تان (mi = 'wine', ‑تان = your [plural/ polite]) so 'your wine' or figuratively 'your revelry'; به شادی بود is 'was for joy' or 'served as joy', and رهنمون is predicate 'guide/guidance' linked by بود ('was') — i.e., 'when your wine was a guide to joy' or 'when in your rejoicing guidance (was present)'. The clause uses past copula بود to describe a state.
segments.s606-g70: بکوشید is the polite/imperative 2nd person plural 'strive/try'. تا introduces the purposive clause 'so that / in order that'. تن is 'the body' (i.e. the person), نگردد is a negative jussive/subjunctive form of 'become' (نگردد = 'do not become'), and زبون is an adjective/noun 'abject, humiliated'. The clause thus means 'strive so that the body does not become humiliated.'
segments.s606-g41: 'به موبد چنین گفت' is 'he said thus to the mobed.' The preposition به + موبد marks the indirect object 'to the mobed'; چنین is an adverb 'thus/so'; گفت is a past tense verb 'said' introducing reported speech; کاین کفشگر begins the reported clause ('this shoemaker').
segments.s606-g42: 'نگه کن' is an imperative 'keep watch / watch!' (کن = imperative 'do'). The conjunction که introduces the subordinate clause, and تا here expresses purpose/extent ('so that / in order to / to see'); از که دارد گهر literally 'from whom he has a jewel' (از = 'from', که = 'who/whom', دارد = 'has', گهر = 'jewel'). Note the classical word order: the prepositional phrase 'از که' precedes the verb دارد and the object گهر follows دارد.
segments.s606-g43: همان مادرش 'that same mother' (همان = 'that/same'; مادر + -ش = 'his/her mother'). چون here functions temporally ('when' / 'as'), so 'چون سخن شد دراز' = 'when the speech became long' — i.e., when she began speaking at length. شد is the past auxiliary/verb 'became', and دراز is the predicate adjective 'long'.
segments.s606-g44: دوان شد بر شاه = 'she ran to the king' (دوان شد = 'ran/ rushed'; بر شاه = 'to/upon the king' with بر marking direction/target). و بگشاد راز = 'and opened/revealed the secret' (بگشاد is the past of 'open' used transitively, taking راز 'secret' as its object).
segments.s606-g45: نخست 'first' (adverb) + آفرین کرد 'uttered praise' (آفرین as 'acclamation/praise' and کرد = 'did/made' — a light-verb construction meaning 'praised') بر شهریار marks the addressee 'to/for the king.' The verb کرد completes the action.
segments.s606-g46: که introduces the content of the praise ('that ...'). شادان بزی is an optative/wish: شادان 'joyful' + بزی an archaic/poetic subjunctive/optative form meaning 'may you live' (addressed to the king). تا بود روزگار = 'as long as the time/day exists' (تا = 'as long as/until', بود = 'is/exists', روزگار = 'time/life'), giving the temporal extent of the wish.
segments.s606-g47: چنین گفت 'thus he said' (چنین = 'thus/so'; گفت = 'said' introducing reported content). کاین نورسیده به جای = 'that this newcomer/inexperienced one in her place...' (کاین = demonstrative 'this/that', نورسیده = 'newly arrived/young/inexperienced', به جای = 'in place of'). This segment sets up the replacement described in the next segment.
segments.s606-g48: یکی زن گزین کرد = 'he chose a certain woman' (یکی = 'one/a certain', زن = 'woman', گزین کرد = 'chose' — گزین is the root 'choose' with کرد used as light-verb). و شد کدخدای = 'and she became kadhkhudā / village headman' (شد = 'became'; کدخدای = 'village headman').
segments.s606-g1: برین‌گونه (literary/poetic adverb) = 'in this manner/thus' placed before the verb to set the scene; بگذشت is past tense 'passed' (impersonal/past narrative: 'it passed'); سالی تمام is a temporal phrase 'a whole year' (سالی = 'year' used as a temporal noun, تمام = 'whole/complete') that follows the verb — so literally 'thus passed a whole year.'
segments.s606-g2: همی is the archaic/poetic imperfective marker meaning 'still/continually'; داشتی here is the past/discontinuous sense of داشتن meaning 'kept/held (a view)' (classical narrative often uses this form to express a continuing past attitude); هرکسی is 'everyone' or 'each person'; می‌حرام is a predicative adjective/phrase 'is forbidden/unclean' — together this reads 'he continued to hold everyone as forbidden.'
segments.s606-g3: همان is a demonstrative 'that/the same'; شه is 'king'; چو is the temporal conjunction 'when/as'; مجلس here means 'court/assembly' (the royal gathering); بیاراستی is a poetic past form meaning 'had set in order/arranged' (classical verb form — the supplied senses list does not include 'arrange', so the exact lexical sense used here is the verb 'to arrange'). The clause means 'when the king arranged the assembly.'
segments.s606-g4: همان repeats 'that' for emphasis; نامه باستان is a noun phrase 'ancient book/record' (نامه here = 'book/record' rather than a short letter); خواستی is past tense 'he sought/wished for' (classical past form). The clause = 'he sought that ancient book.'
segments.s606-g5: چنین بود تا is a narrative formula 'thus it was until…' where تا introduces the limiting temporal clause; کودکی here functions as 'a child' (poetic noun used as subject) and stands directly before کفشگر to indicate 'a shoemaker's child' by juxtaposition (classical Persian often omits the ezāfe in poetry).
segments.s606-g6: زنی is the direct object 'a woman'; خواست is the past tense of خواستن 'sought/wanted' (verb comes after object and completes the SOV structure); با introduces a list of desirable qualities 'with'; چیز here means 'possessions/things' (i.e., material means), نام means 'name' used as 'reputation/fame', and گهر is 'a gem/jewel' — the prepositional phrase modifies the woman sought.
segments.s606-g7: نبودش is an existential/possessive negation: نبود + pronominal suffix ش = 'it/for him was not' → 'he did not have'; دران = 'in/for that' (poetic compound of در + آن); کار افزار is a compound 'work-tools' = 'tools/equipment for the work' (noun‑noun compound typical in classical style); سخت is an adjective 'hard/difficult' that qualifies the work or the tools. Together: 'He did not have the tools for that hard work.'
segments.s606-g8: همی again marks continued or emphatic action 'still/continually'; زار used adverbially/verbally means 'lamentingly/bitterly' or 'in a state of wretchedness'; بگریست is past 'wept/wailed'; مامش is the pronominal 'his/her mother' (colloquial form); ز is the poetic preposition 'from/because of' and بخت means 'fortune/fate' — 'his mother wailed and wept because of (his) fate.' Note the poetic inversion (verb before subject) is common in Ferdowsi.
segments.s606-g49: به کار اندرون is a prepositional/adverbial phrase ('in/for the work of the inner quarters') placed before the subject. نایژه is the (agent) noun ‘nursemaid’ (here the subject) and سست بود is the past copula + adjective predicate ('was lax/weak'). The order is typical Persian: adverbial phrase → subject → predicate (adjective + بود, past 'to be').
segments.s606-g50: دلش is the possessed noun 'her/his heart' (دل + possessive ش). گفتی here is a parenthetical/colloquial particle meaning 'so to speak / one might say' (archaic/useful as a hedge) that does not behave like the lexical noun 'conversation'. از introduces the cause or source ('because of / from'), modifying خودرست (headstrong) which together with بود forms the predicate 'was headstrong because of weakness'.
segments.s606-g51: بدادم is a past verbal form ('gave') used here in narrative; the grammatical subject is omitted (context supplies it). سه جام نبیدش نهان is a noun phrase 'three cups of her nabīd (wine) secretly' where نبیدش is 'nabīd' + possessive and نهان qualifies the action as hidden/secret. The whole phrase functions as the object of the past verb.
segments.s606-g52: که introduces a subordinate clause of result/reason ('so that / such that' or 'that'). ماند is the past/present-stative 'remained/was left' and کس here functions as an indefinite often with negative force in context ('no one / anyone'); از تخم او در جهان is a prepositional phrase 'from his/her seed in the world' specifying what did or did not remain.
segments.s606-g53: هم‌اندر زمان is an adverbial phrase 'at the same time / likewise at that time'. لعل (literally 'ruby', here a poetic image) is the noun undergoing the change; گشتش is the past/telic verb 'it became/turned' with the enclitic ش referring back to the noun, and رخان is the adjectival predicate 'relaxed/loose' describing the change. Word order: temporal phrase → topic → verb+enclitic → adjective predicate.
segments.s606-g54: نمد سر برآورد is a figurative clause where نمد (a felt covering) is the subject and سر برآورد is a light-verb construction meaning 'raised its head' (سر + برآورد). The conjunction و links this to گشت استخوان, where the verb گشت 'became' takes استخوان 'bone' as its predicate complement; the subject is carried over from the previous clause (ellipsis).
segments.s606-g55: نژادش is the possessed noun 'his/her lineage'. نبد is an archaic negative copular/verb meaning 'was none / did not exist', so the clause says 'his lineage was nothing except ...'. جز introduces the exception 'except for' followed by the noun phrase 'سه جام نبید' as the only remainder.
segments.s606-g56: که introduces a content clause 'that'. دانست is the past tense 'he knew', and the following subordinate clause کاین شاه خواهد شنید is future-in-the-past reported content: کاین ('that this') + شاه 'the king' + خواهد (future auxiliary) + شنید 'will hear'. Together they mean 'he knew that the king would hear of this.'
segments.s606-g33: VSO order: 'یکی کفشگر دید' = 'he saw a certain shoemaker.' 'یکی' before the noun here means 'a certain' (not the numeral emphasis). 'دید' is the simple past 'saw' and takes the following descriptive phrase as what was seen; 'بر پشت شیر' is a prepositional phrase 'on the back of the lion' (بر = on, پشت = back, شیر = lion).
segments.s606-g34: 'نشسته' is a past-participial/adjectival form meaning 'sitting' and modifies the shoemaker mentioned just before. 'چو' introduces a simile 'like/as', so 'چو بر خر سواری دلیر' reads 'like a brave rider on a donkey'; 'بر خر' = 'on a donkey', 'سواری' = 'rider/ride', and 'دلیر' = 'brave' describes the rider.
segments.s606-g35: 'بیامد دوان' is a compound indicating motion — 'he came running' (بیامد = came, دوان = running/rushed). 'تا' here marks the endpoint/extent 'up to' or 'as far as', so 'تا در بارگاه' = 'as far as into the palace/court'.
segments.s606-g36: 'دلیر' functions as the subject label 'the brave one' (the bold fellow). 'اندر آمد' is the literary phrasing for 'entered/ came in', and 'به نزدیک شاه' indicates movement toward proximity to the king ('به' = toward, 'نزدیک' = near, 'شاه' = king).
segments.s606-g37: 'بگفت' introduces reported speech or narration ('he said' / 'he recounted'). 'آن دلیری' is 'that bravery/deed of valor' as the object, and 'کزو دیده بود' is a relative clause 'which he had seen' where 'کزو' = 'from which/which' and 'دیده بود' is past perfect ('had seen').
segments.s606-g38: 'به دیده بدید' literally 'he saw with (his) eye(s)' — 'به' used adverbially ('by/with'), 'دیده' = 'eye', and 'بدید' = simple past 'saw'. 'آنچ نشنیده بود' = 'that which he had not heard' (آنچ = that which; نشنیده بود = 'had not heard'). The clause contrasts direct sight with prior hearsay.
segments.s606-g39: 'جهاندار' is the title 'the sovereign/king'. 'زان' = 'because of that / from that', and 'در شگفتی بماند' = 'remained in astonishment' (در شگفتی = in astonishment; بماند = remained/lingered — narrative past sense in this context).
segments.s606-g40: 'همه موبدان و ردان را بخواند' is a simple transitive clause: 'he called/summoned all the mobeds and the rādān.' 'همه' = all, 'موبدان' = mobeds (Zoroastrian priests), 'و' = and, 'ردان' is another group of men being summoned, 'را' marks the definite object, and 'بخواند' here means 'summoned/called' (classical past narrative).
segments.s606-g25: بشد تیز is a two-part verbal phrase: بشد is past tense 'became/grew' and together with the adjective تیز ('swift') gives the sense 'he became swift / he rushed'. The coordinating و links this action to the following clause. بر شیر غران نشست is a normal past clause: بر + noun ('on the') + postpositive adjective غران ('roaring') modifies شیر ('lion'), and نشست is the past of 'to sit/settle' meaning 'he sat on / leaped upon (the lion)'.
segments.s606-g26: بیازید is used as a past intransitive verb here meaning 'he opened/gaped' (literal 'opened his mouth' implied). The conjunction و links to بگرفت ('he seized'), another past verb. گوشش is the noun 'ear' plus the enclitic -ش 'his/its', and به دست functions as an instrumental phrase 'with (his) hand' (به used here to mark means).
segments.s606-g27: بران is a locative/retroactive pointer 'upon that' referring back to the previously mentioned lion. شیر غران is a noun + postpositive adjective 'roaring lion'. پسر شیر بود is a nominal predicate with بود ('was') stating identity/possession: 'was the son of the lion.' Word order places the descriptive particle (بران) before the noun phrase.
segments.s606-g28: جوان 'a young one' is the subject; از + بر here combine to indicate position 'on/above' (classical Persian uses such stacked particles for location). The conjunction و links the second clause شیر در زیر بود, where در زیر ('in/underneath') marks the lion's position and بود is the past copula 'was'. The overall sense contrasts the young one on top with the lion beneath.
segments.s606-g29: همی شد دوان is a periphrastic past/progressive: همی (continuous emphasis) + شد ('became' / auxiliary) + دوان ('running') gives 'kept running' or 'ran off'. The subject شیروان follows the verb (VSO order is common); چون نوند is a simile 'like Nund' (چون = 'like').
segments.s606-g30: This is an instrumental/telegraphic phrase modifying the previous verb: به یک دست marks the instrument/position 'with one hand' (به used to mark means), followed by the objects زنجیر 'chain' and, with و دیگر 'and the other (hand)', کمند 'lasso'. The verb 'held' is omitted (understood from context).
segments.s606-g31: چو is the subordinating conjunction 'when'. آن is the demonstrative 'that'. شیربان is a noun/epithet (literally 'milk-man' or 'nourisher' in the lexicon but here an epithet/title), followed by the honorific/epithet جهاندار ('world-holder/ruler') and شاه 'king' — a string of titles in apposition forming the subject noun phrase.
segments.s606-g32: بیامد is the past 'came'. ز (poetic form of از) means 'from', خانه is 'the house', and the sequence ب + دان corresponds to بدَان ('to that') where ب- marks motion 'to/into' and دان here functions as part of the deictic 'that' (no matching sense provided in the lemma list). جایگاه is 'place/spot'. The whole clause reads 'came from the house to that place.'
segments.s606-g57: This is a compact clause in which the verb بخندید (past tense, 'laughed') comes first for poetic emphasis, followed by the demonstrative/relational زان + noun پیرزن ('that old woman'). شاه is the subject ('the king') and گفت is the reporting verb. Word order is somewhat inverted for style (V + prepositional/demonstrative phrase + subject + speech-verb).
segments.s606-g58: که introduces a content clause ('that'). این داستان را is the direct object with را marking definiteness/emphasis after داستان. نشاید is an impersonal/modal present form meaning 'it is not fitting/appropriate' and نهفت is used as a participial/adjectival verb meaning 'hidden' (so together: 'that this story should not be hidden').
segments.s606-g59: به موبد چنین گفت is an introductory speech formula: به + indirect object (موبد, 'the mobad/priest') marks the addressee, چنین ('thus/so') modifies the manner of speech, and گفت is the speech verb. The adverbial/temporal کاکنون ('ka-konun', archaic compound) attaches to the following noun نبید to mean 'now/well, as for now, wine'.
segments.s606-g60: حلالست is copular (حلال + ـست) meaning 'it is lawful.' The noun میخواره ('wine‑drinker') is the pragmatic subject/agent to be chosen; باید is the modal 'must/ought' and گزید is the past stem of گزیدن used after باید to express obligation ('must choose' or 'one must choose'). The clause reads as a prescription: 'wine is lawful; a drinker must be chosen.'
segments.s606-g61: که introduces a result/consequence clause ('so that/that'), چندان is an adverb meaning 'to such an extent' or 'so much', and خورد is the simple past/past-stem 'he drank' (here part of a result construction). می‌که is the archaic/poetic compound for 'the wine which/that' (می = wine, with relative/connector که). بر + نره + شیر is a prepositional phrase 'upon the male lion' (نره specifying male). The phrase sets up a degree/result relation: 'that he drank so much wine that ...'.
segments.s606-g62: نشیند is a present/subjunctive form of 'to sit' (3rd person) used in a resultive clause ('would sit'). نیارد here functions as an archaic negative/resultive verb meaning 'would not bring/would not cause' (often written نیاورد in later Persian). ورا = 'to him/thither' marks the target, and شیر زیر = 'a lion beneath / lion under (him)'—the final phrase completes the result notion. Grammatically this segment completes the 'so that' clause begun before: 'so that ... a lion would not make/put him under.'
segments.s606-g63: نه چندان que/phrase 'not so much' (negating the degree). که again introduces a subordinate clause; چشمش ('his eye') is a possessive construction (چشم + ـش). کلاغ سیاه is a noun phrase 'black crow' — together the image is idiomatic/poetic: 'not to the extent that his eye would (even) pluck out a black crow.'
segments.s606-g64: همی is a frequentative/continuous adverb (here 'would/continually' or emphatic), followed by برکند (verb 'to pluck/tear away' in present/resultive sense) describing the action of the eye in the idiom. رفته is the perfect/participial 'having gone' (here 'had gone / departed'), and از نزد شاه is a prepositional phrase 'from the presence of the king' (نزد meaning 'in the presence of'). The clause therefore ends the line with 'he had gone from the king's presence.'
segments.s606-g9: همانا is an emphatic adverb meaning “truly/verily” placed at the start to signal emphasis. نهان داشت is a light-verb construction: نهان (adverbial/participial “secretly/hidden”) + داشت (past-tense ‘had/kept’) = “kept hidden.” لختی modifies نبید and means “for a short while,” so لختی نبید is the object phrase ‘the wine for a little while.’
segments.s606-g10: پسر را uses را as the definite/direct-object marker (the youth as definite object). The cluster ب + دان here functions like classical به + آن (“to/into that”), with دان bound to the prefix; خانه اندر is the locative phrase “into/within that house” using the older preposition اندر. کشید is a past tense verb ‘drew/dragged/led’ placed at the end, agreeing with the usual SOV order in classical Persian.
segments.s606-g11: به پور جوان گفت is an address-construction: به + addressee (“to the young son/young man”) + گفت (past ‘said’). کاین is a contracted relative/particle (classical که + این) introducing the content clause “that these …”; هفت جام is the object of the reported speech (‘these seven cups’).
segments.s606-g12: بخور is the 2nd-person imperative of خوردن (‘eat/drink’). تا introduces a purpose/result clause here meaning “so that/in order that.” شوی is the present/subjunctive form of شدن, used after تا to express the desired result (“you may become/you be”), and ایمن و شادکام are predicate adjectives (‘safe and fortunate’).
segments.s606-g13: مگر here introduces an exception/conditional ‘unless.’ بشکنی is the subjunctive/optative 2nd-person form of شکستن (‘you break’), typical after مگر in conditional clauses. امشب is a temporal adverb ‘tonight,’ and آن مهر تنگ is the definite object phrase ‘that tight seal’ (مهر used as ‘seal’ and تنگ as ‘tight, narrow’).
segments.s606-g14: کلنگ از نمد is an ablative construction ‘a pickaxe from/out of felt’ (meaning a pickaxe made of felt). کی کند is a rhetorical question — کی here functions as a temporal/rhetorical interrogative (‘when/how’) with کند (present/optative ‘does/makes’) to mean ‘how/when would it do so?’ کان سنگ is an archaic phrasing; کان here has a figurative/relational force and سنگ is ‘stone’ — the sense is rhetorical impossibility (‘make into stone’).
segments.s606-g15: بزد is the simple past ‘he struck/smote.’ The following noun کفشگر identifies the agent/figure (the shoemaker). جام می‌هفت و هشت reads as a compound: جام (cup) plus the archaic/measured phrase می‌هفت (‘the seventh draught’) و هشت (‘and eighth’), so the phrase refers to the seventh and eighth cups/draughts. Word order is compressed and epic/poetic, with the verb up front and its objects/adjuncts following.
segments.s606-g16: هم‌اندر زمان is an adverbial phrase ‘at the same time / likewise.’ آتشش is a possessed noun ‘his fire’ (–ش = his), used metaphorically for passion/ardor. سخت گشت is a two-word predicate: سخت (adverb/adjective ‘severe/strong’) + گشت (past of گشتن ‘became’), giving ‘became intense/severe.’
segments.s606-g17: جوانمرد را (object marked) places 'the young man / brave one' as the affected person; جام is the grammatical subject 'the cup' (i.e., the wine) and گستاخ کرد is a causative/perfective clause meaning 'made (him) insolent/bold.' In other words, 'the cup made the young hero audacious.' Note that کرد here functions as 'to cause/make.'
segments.s606-g18: بیامد is a simple past 'he came'; the omitted subject is the young man from the previous clause. در خانه سوراخ کرد is a verbal phrase with در خانه ('into / in the house') followed by سوراخ کرد 'made/broke a hole' (کرد = 'to make'). The sequence describes his next action after being made bold by the cup.
segments.s606-g19: وزان ('from that' / 'and from that') is a connective adverb introducing what follows; جایگه شد is a copular construction where جایگه (place/position) precedes شد 'became/assumed' meaning 'he assumed that place.' به درگاه خویش is a prepositional phrase 'to/at his court/threshold' where خوی + ش is a possessive formation meaning 'his own.'
segments.s606-g20: شده is a perfect/past-participle form functioning like 'having become' or 'became'; it heads a series of participial/resultative predicates. شاددل 'cheerful' is an adjective complement to شده, and یافته راه خویش is a participial clause 'having found his way' (یافته = 'found', راه خویش = 'his path'). The two predicates are coordinated, describing his restored state.
segments.s606-g21: چنان ... که is a correlative construction 'so ... that': چنان 'so' + بد 'bad/severe' + که 'that' introduces the result-clause. از خانه شیران شاه is a genitival phrase 'from the house/den of the king's lions' (شیران here 'lions' / figurative warriors). The clause sets up the result that follows.
segments.s606-g22: یکی شیر 'one (lion)' is the subject; بگسست is a past intransitive verb meaning 'broke loose / burst forth' (بـ- prefix + گسست); و آمد به راه 'and came onto the road' continues the narrative. The verbs are simple past, and the sequence describes the lion's departure.
segments.s606-g23: ازان 'from that' introduces the cause; می‌همی is an archaic/poetic form referring to the wine/draught (here 'that drinking'); کفشگر مست بود is a straightforward past clause 'the shoemaker was drunk.' The whole segment gives the reason for his impaired perception.
segments.s606-g24: به دیده ندید is literal 'with (his) eye(s) he did not see' (دیده = 'eye'); آنچ 'that which' is a relative/indefinite pronoun and بایست بود is a modal/configurational phrase 'ought to have been / should have been.' Together the clause means 'he did not see with his eyes that which ought to have been (seen).' Note that بایست is modal 'ought' and بود is past copula.

sentences.s606-s33: A cry arose then from the doorway: "O golden-girt champions!"
sentences.s606-s34: You will receive according to each person's measure; consider your own beginning and end.
sentences.s606-s35: When your wine was for joy (or your merriment brought joy) and acted as guide, strive so that the body does not become abject.
sentences.s606-s21: He said to the mobed thus: "Watch this shoemaker — keep an eye on him to see from whom he has the jewel."
sentences.s606-s22: When that mother’s speech grew long, she ran to the king and disclosed the secret.
sentences.s606-s23: First she praised the sovereign, saying, 'May you live joyfully as long as time endures.'
sentences.s606-s24: Thus he said that he chose a woman in place of this newcomer, and she became the village headman.
sentences.s606-s1: Thus a whole year passed, and he continued to regard everyone as forbidden/unclean.
sentences.s606-s2: When the king had arranged the assembly, he sought that ancient book.
sentences.s606-s3: Thus it remained until a shoemaker's child sought a woman with possessions, reputation, and a jewel.
sentences.s606-s4: He lacked the tools for that hard work; his mother bewailed him bitterly because of fate.
sentences.s606-s25: The nursemaid in charge of the inner household was lax; her heart, so to speak, was headstrong because of that weakness.
sentences.s606-s26: I secretly gave her three cups of nabīd (wine), so that no one of her seed would remain in the world.
sentences.s606-s27: At the same time the ruby (liveliness) became slack; the felt lifted its head and became bone.
sentences.s606-s28: His lineage came to nothing except those three cups of nabīd, for he knew that the king would hear of this.
sentences.s606-s17: He saw a certain shoemaker sitting on the lion's back, like a brave rider on a donkey.
sentences.s606-s18: He came running up to the royal court; the brave one entered and approached near the king.
sentences.s606-s19: The brave man spoke of that bravery which he had seen — he saw with his own eyes that which he had not merely heard of.
sentences.s606-s20: The sovereign remained astonished at that; he summoned all the mobeds and the rādān.
sentences.s606-s13: He rushed forward and leaped onto the roaring lion; he opened his mouth and seized its ear in his hand.
sentences.s606-s14: On that roaring lion was the lion's son; the young one was on top and the lion was beneath.
sentences.s606-s15: The Shirvan ran swiftly like Nund, holding a chain in one hand and a lasso in the other.
sentences.s606-s16: When that lion-keeper, the world-ruling king, came from the house to that place.
sentences.s606-s29: The king laughed at that old woman and said that this story ought not to be hidden.
sentences.s606-s30: He said to the mobad thus: 'Now wine is lawful; one must choose a drinker.'
sentences.s606-s31: so that he drink so much wine that a male lion would not sit upon him, nor would a lion bring him beneath (its power).
sentences.s606-s32: Not so much that his eye would pluck out a black crow; he had departed from the king's presence.
sentences.s606-s5: Indeed, he kept the wine hidden for a short while and drew the youth into that house.
sentences.s606-s6: He said to the young man, “Drink these seven cups so that you may be safe and fortunate.”
sentences.s606-s7: Unless you break tonight that tight seal, how can a pickaxe of felt make (it) into stone?
sentences.s606-s8: The shoemaker struck; (he) took/drank the seventh and eighth cup, and at the same time his fire became intense.
sentences.s606-s9: The cup made the young champion insolent; he came and made a hole in the house.
sentences.s606-s10: From that place he took his seat at his own court; he grew cheerful and found his own path.
sentences.s606-s11: So (bad) it was that from the house of the king's lions one lion broke loose and came onto the road.
sentences.s606-s12: From that draught the shoemaker was drunk; with his eyes he did not see what ought to have been seen.
